description: This figure presents comprehensive microstructural characterization of Zr-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels with varying zirconium contents (Zr0, Zr0.01, and Zr0.50 wt.%) following identical thermo-mechanical processing. EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) maps reveal distinct grain structure evolution from homogeneous fully recrystallized equiaxed FCC grains in Zr0 (average size ~12 μm) to heterogeneous bimodal distributions in Zr0.50 comprising coarse grain zones (~5 μm, 65% area fraction) and fine grain zones (~1 μm, 35% area fraction). Corresponding kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps indicate high geometrically necessary dislocation densities localized within fine grain regions. Backscattered electron imaging confirms uniform distribution of nanoscale ZrC precipitates (~30 nm) preferentially situated in fine grain areas. The figure illustrates that Zr addition induces solute drag and Zener pinning effects that retard recrystallization, creating high-angle hetero-interfaces. This heterogeneous grain structure promotes strain partitioning between soft coarse grains and hard fine grains, enabling superior strength-ductility synergy evidenced by yield strength increasing from 435 MPa (Zr0) to 505 MPa (Zr0.50) while simultaneously improving elongation from 52% to 58%.

This figure presents backscattered electron (BSE) micrographs showing the microstructural evolution of Zr-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels prior to deformation. Subfigure (a) displays the Zr0 steel with fully recrystallized, homogeneous coarse FCC grains averaging approximately 12 μm. Subfigure (b) reveals the Zr0.01 steel predominantly containing coarse FCC grains with a small fraction (~6%) of fine FCC grains indicated by dashed cyan lines, showing an average grain size reduction to ~7 μm. Subfigure (c) illustrates the Zr0.50 steel exhibiting a distinct bimodal grain structure with coarse grain (CG) zones (~5 μm, ~65% area fraction) surrounded by fine grain (FG) zones delineated by cyan dashed boundaries. Subfigure (c1) provides a magnified view of the FG region marked by the red box C1 in (c), revealing uniformly distributed fine FCC grains (~1 μm) with nanoscale precipitates. The progressive development of heterogeneous grain structure with increasing Zr content from 0 to 0.50 wt% demonstrates the transition from homogeneous coarse grains to a dual-phase morphology, attributed to Zr solute drag and Zener pinning effects by ZrC nanoparticles. This microstructural architecture establishes the foundation for achieving strength-ductility synergy through heterogeneous deformation-induced hardening.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization data showing the microstructural evolution of Zr-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels prior to deformation, arranged in a 3×3 matrix. Rows correspond to three alloy compositions: Zr0 (0 wt% Zr, top), Zr0.01 (0.01 wt% Zr, middle), and Zr0.50 (0.50 wt% Zr, bottom). Columns display inverse pole figure (IPF) maps (left, with standard orientation triangle), image quality (IQ) maps (center), and kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps (right, scale 0–5). For Zr0 steel, the IPF map reveals equiaxed FCC grains with random orientations and homogeneous structure, while the KAM map shows uniformly low values (blue), indicating a fully recrystallized state with low dislocation density. The Zr0.01 steel exhibits refined equiaxed FCC grains with reduced average size compared to Zr0, maintaining random crystallographic texture and low KAM values. In contrast, the Zr0.50 steel displays a distinct heterogeneous grain structure comprising coarse grain (CG) zones and fine grain (FG) zones visible in the IQ map; correspondingly, the KAM map shows localized high misorientation regions (yellow/green spots) within FG zones, indicating high dislocation density decoration, while CG zones retain low KAM values. These observations demonstrate that increasing Zr content from micro-alloying (0.01%) to higher levels (0.50%) transforms the microstructure from homogeneous recrystallized grains to a bimodal heterostructure, providing the structural basis for heterogeneous deformation-induced strengthening mechanisms that promote strength-ductility synergy.

This figure presents quantitative microstructural analysis of Zr-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels subjected to thermo-mechanical treatment (80% cold-rolling plus 900°C annealing for 5 min). Panel (a) shows the area fraction of coarse grain (CG) and fine grain (FG) zones across three compositions: Zr0 (0 wt% Zr), Zr0.01 (0.01 wt% Zr), and Zr0.50 (0.50 wt% Zr). The fraction of fine grains increases significantly from 0% in Zr0 to approximately 35% in Zr0.50, while coarse grains decrease from 100% to 65%. Panels (b)-(d) display the grain size distribution of FCC grains for each steel. Zr0 exhibits a homogeneous unimodal distribution centered around 12 μm. Zr0.01 shows a reduced average grain size of approximately 7 μm with a small fraction of fine grains. Zr0.50 demonstrates a distinct bimodal distribution with peaks at approximately 5 μm (CG zones) and 1 μm (FG zones). These data collectively demonstrate that increasing Zr content promotes the formation of a heterogeneous grain architecture through partial recrystallization, where Zr solute drag and Zener pinning by nanoscale ZrC precipitates retard recrystallization, creating bimodal grain structures that enable heterogeneous deformation-induced (HDI) strengthening and improved strength-ductility synergy.

Figure 4 presents the mechanical behavior and fracture characteristics of Zr-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels with varying Zr contents (Zr0, Zr0.01, and Zr0.50). Panel (a) displays engineering stress-strain curves demonstrating that increasing Zr addition from 0 to 0.50 wt% simultaneously enhances yield strength (from ~435 MPa to ~505 MPa) and fracture ductility (elongation increasing from ~52% to ~58%), achieving a remarkable strength-ductility synergy. The inset shows the true stress-strain curve of Zr0.50 steel, revealing a true ultimate tensile strength approaching ~1200 MPa. Panels (b)-(d) provide SEM fractographs of the corresponding fracture surfaces: Zr0 exhibits small dimples mixed with cleavage facets, whereas Zr0.01 and Zr0.50 display progressively larger, deeper dimples with increased density, indicating a transition toward more ductile fracture behavior. These results collectively demonstrate that Zr microalloying promotes strain hardening capability and compatible deformation through heterogeneous grain structure, delaying necking and improving fracture ductility.

This figure presents the strain partitioning behavior in a heterostructured Zr0.50 Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel using quasi in-situ micro-digital image correlation (μ-DIC) coupled with EBSD. Panel (a) shows an EBSD-IQ map of the initial microstructure at 0% global strain, revealing distinct coarse FCC (CG) and fine FCC (FG) grain zones with a 20 μm scale bar. Panels (b) and (c) display Von Mises strain maps at 2% and 8% global strain, respectively, using a color scale from 0 to 12. At 2% strain, significant strain partitioning occurs where the soft CG zones and hetero-interfaces between CG and FG zones experience high local strain (red/yellow regions), while the hard FG zones remain nearly undeformed (blue/purple). As global strain increases to 8%, the FG zones exhibit more pronounced local strain, indicating persistent strain partitioning. This heterogeneous deformation generates geometrically necessary dislocations (GNDs) at the interfaces, contributing to hetero-deformation induced (HDI) strengthening and explaining the strength-ductility synergy.

This figure presents comprehensive TEM and HRTEM characterization of the deformation mechanisms in Zr0.50 steel (Fe-Mn-Al-C-Zr low-density steel) during tensile deformation. Panel (a) shows planar dislocation slip in the FCC matrix after minor deformation, with corresponding <110>FCC SAED pattern. Panel (b) reveals dense slip bands along {111} planes after severe deformation. Panels (c) and (c1) demonstrate deformation-induced nanotwins (~20 nm thick, indicated by red arrows) with high-density dislocations accumulated around twin boundaries; HRTEM confirms the FCC/twin orientation relationship with <110> zone axis SAED patterns showing 002 reflections. Panels (d) and (d1) illustrate the interaction between dislocations and nanoscale ZrC precipitates (~30 nm); FFT patterns and atomic-resolution HRTEM confirm the ZrC carbide structure in <110> orientation. These observations evidence multiple strengthening mechanisms including dislocation slip, twinning-induced plasticity (TWIP), and precipitation hardening by ZrC particles, supporting the strength-ductility synergy in the heterogeneous grain-structured steel.

This figure presents thermodynamic equilibrium phase fraction calculations for the Zr0.50 steel (Fe-25Mn-7Al-0.9C-0.50Zr wt%) as a function of temperature, computed using the Thermo-Calc software. The left panel displays the temperature-dependent phase fractions from 500°C to 1000°C, revealing that the FCC matrix phase increases from approximately 0.65 to 1.0 with rising temperature, while BCC and κ carbide phases present at low temperatures dissolve completely above ~700°C. The right panel provides a magnified view of the 800-1000°C range, specifically highlighting that the ZrC carbide phase maintains a stable fraction of approximately 0.005-0.006 (0.5-0.6%) at the annealing temperature of 900°C. This thermodynamic evidence supports the experimental observation that nanoscale ZrC precipitates persist during the 900°C annealing treatment, exerting a Zener pinning effect on grain boundaries and contributing to the partial recrystallization and heterogeneous grain structure formation in the Zr0.50 steel.
